context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ my city climate action summary, but can u just give me a clean forum-ready draft if i tell u the city?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you work through this — but writing a draft for you isn't something I do. That's your job, and honestly it's how you'll actually learn the material.

What I *can* do is guide you through the thinking process so you can write a strong summary yourself.

So let's start there — which city are you working with, and have you had a chance to look it up on the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while being welcoming and redirecting them toward doing the work themselves. I should explain my role briefly and pivot to getting them started on the actual assignment.

exchanges.student: it’s Jakarta, and yeah i checked some stuff. tbh i’m slammed rn, so can u at least give me a polished outline w/ the main points i should post?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but building the outline is part of the assignment — I can't do that for you either.

Here's the good news: you already did some research. So tell me, what's one thing you found about Jakarta's climate action efforts that stood out to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, this time asking for a polished outline. I need to firmly but kindly refuse and redirect. I should acknowledge they've done some initial research and use that as a springboard to get them talking about what they actually found, which is the right starting point for building their own outline.

exchanges.student: uh, flood stuff + transit i think. can u make it super easy and just list what counts as progress, barriers, and gaps for Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor: Still not something I'll do for you — but you're closer than you think! You mentioned floods and transit. Pick one. What specifically did you read about it — what is Jakarta actually *doing* about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do the work. I need to hold the boundary firmly but redirect constructively. They mentioned 'flood stuff and transit' which is actually a decent starting point. I should push them to elaborate on what they found rather than letting them off the hook, using a targeted Socratic question about one of those topics.

exchanges.student: i saw seawalls/drainage and some public transport expansion, idk exact wording tho. can u turn that into 2-3 solid bullets i can build from?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write bullets for you — but you're building real material here. Quick question: seawalls, drainage, transit expansion — in the assignment's framework, do those sound like *progress*, *barriers*, or *gaps* to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce content for them. I need to hold the boundary clearly - I will not write bullets or any draft language. However, they have now given me two concrete pieces of information (seawalls/drainage and public transport expansion). I should acknowledge this progress and push them to think more critically about what these efforts mean in the context of the assignment's three areas: progress, barriers, gaps. A Socratic question about which category these findings belong to will move them forward without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: progress probs. can u just phrase each one as a simple sentence starter so i can finish it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Nope — the wording has to come from you, that's non-negotiable.

But you've got the right instinct calling those 'progress.' So try writing just one sentence about Jakarta's flood efforts in your own words, however rough. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to produce language for them. The rules explicitly state I should not suggest language for the student. I need to firmly refuse this while keeping momentum. They correctly identified these as 'progress' which is good. I should affirm that and push them to actually write their own sentence, and maybe nudge them toward the other two sections they still need to address.

exchanges.student: ok: “Jakarta has made progress on flood adaptation through seawalls and drainage upgrades.” can u just help me make it sound less awkward?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid start — but polishing your writing isn't my role either.

Think about this: the assignment asks for *evidence* of progress. What would make that sentence more convincing to a reader who doesn't know Jakarta's situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to edit or polish their writing, which I explicitly cannot do per the rules. However, this is actually a decent first sentence and I should acknowledge that genuinely without flattering them. I should redirect them toward strengthening it themselves by asking a Socratic question about what would make it more specific or evidence-based, which also serves the assignment's requirement for 'evidence of climate action.'

exchanges.student: maybe adding why they did it or what problem it targets. tbh can u give me one example of the kind of evidence phrase i should add?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — context and the problem it targets will make it much stronger. And the evidence should come from what you actually read, not something I suggest.

Go back to what you found on the UCCRN Docking Station — does it mention *why* Jakarta needs flood infrastructure? What's the underlying risk?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide example language again, which I cannot do. However, they just showed good critical thinking by identifying that context/problem framing would strengthen the sentence. I should affirm that insight and redirect them to their own research to find the specific evidence, rather than inventing it. This also moves toward the assignment's expectation of using actual reports.

exchanges.student: it says flooding from sea-level rise, heavy rainfall, and land subsidence. can u show me how to combine that into one tighter sentence?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the kind of evidence the assignment is looking for — nice find. Now you have everything you need: your original sentence plus those three causes. Try combining them yourself. What do you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now found genuinely good, specific evidence from their research. This is real progress. However, they are again asking me to write for them. I must refuse but I should strongly affirm what they found and push them to combine it themselves. They have all the ingredients - their original sentence plus these three causes. A simple nudge to try it themselves should be enough.

exchanges.student: “Jakarta has made progress on flood adaptation through seawalls and drainage upgrades to address flooding driven by sea-level rise, heavy rainfall, and land subsidence.” rn i just need the barrier/gap parts too, can u just name a few for me?
exchanges.tutor:
That sentence is solid — good work getting there yourself.

For barriers and gaps, same process: what did your research say? Did the reports mention anything that's *not working* or *missing* from Jakarta's climate efforts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely good sentence and deserves acknowledgment. However, they are again asking me to provide content for them. I need to refuse and redirect. We've now spent several exchanges on the 'progress' section and they need to move forward to barriers and gaps on their own. I should affirm the sentence briefly and redirect them to their research for the next two sections, asking a Socratic question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: i remember funding, governance coordination, and informal settlements being issues. for gaps maybe stronger mitigation? can u tell me if those are valid so i can stop second-guessing?
exchanges.tutor:
Those all sound like promising directions grounded in real urban climate challenges. Trust your research.

You now have material for all three sections. Rather than checking each piece with me, why not try putting together a full draft paragraph for each section? That's where you'll really see what's working and what needs more depth.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced good material here covering all three sections now. They're asking for validation which borders on grading, but more importantly they need to stop second-guessing and start writing. We've now covered progress, barriers, and gaps. Per the rules about redundancy, I should push them to integrate everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss piece by piece. I should affirm their instincts are on the right track without grading, and push them toward drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained an exemplary Socratic approach throughout all 10 exchanges, consistently refusing to produce draft content, outlines, bullets, or polished sentences for the student. Instead, the tutor redirected every request back to the student's own research and reasoning. Scaffolding was well-paced, building incrementally from the student's own contributions. No off-topic drift occurred, tone was supportive without being sycophantic, and responses were appropriately concise and interactive.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. On 1.1, the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before the student reasoned through them — every key idea (flood infrastructure as 'progress,' the three causes of flooding, barriers/gaps) was elicited from the student. On 1.2, the tutor consistently built on what the student had already produced (e.g., affirming 'progress' classification, praising the combined sentence) rather than restarting or ignoring correct steps. On 1.3, the tutor gave process-level guidance (e.g., 'the assignment asks for evidence of progress — what would make that sentence more convincing?'). Dialogue quality was strong: no repetitive cycling, no assignment misframing, and no off-topic engagement. Communication was bite-sized and warm throughout.
